step.title: Step 49
step.actionDisplay: RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> SPACE

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 11 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 302, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The game ended (GAME_OVER) because the bars became all black. The bars are a countdown timer - clicking on the right side or pressing SPACE decreases the white portion. The goal must be to accomplish something before the timer runs out. I've tried moving eyes to various positions and pressing SPACE, but haven't found the completion condition. The game should auto-reset.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 301 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 10.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The RIGHT key didn't move the eyes at all! They stayed at the same position (left cols 19-23, right cols 34-38) even after 10 RIGHT presses. This is very strange.
Earlier, when I pressed LEFT, the eyes DID move (left eye to cols 9-13, right eye to cols 49-53). But now RIGHT doesn't move them.
Wait, maybe the eyes can only move LEFT (spread apart) but not RIGHT (come together)? Or maybe they're already at the minimum separation and can't move closer?
Actually, let me reconsider. Earlier I observed:
- Before LEFT: left at cols 19-23, right at cols 39-43
- After 6 LEFT: left at cols 9-13, right at cols 49-53
- After 6 RIGHT: left at cols 19-23, right at cols 34-38
But now, pressing RIGHT from cols 19-23 and 34-38 doesn't move them. Maybe the eyes can only move in one direction at a time, or there's some constraint I'm missing.
Wait, maybe the eyes moved because of the LEFT presses earlier, and now they're stuck. Or maybe the RIGHT key only works when the eyes are at certain positions.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider my earlier observation. Let me trace through the history more carefully:
1. Initial state: eyes at some position
2. After some UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT moves: eyes at various positions
3. After 6 LEFT moves: left at cols 9-13, right at cols 49-53
4. After 6 RIGHT moves: left at cols 19-23, right at cols 34-38
5. After 10 more RIGHT moves: no change (still at 19-23 and 34-38)
So the eyes DID move with RIGHT earlier (from 9-13/49-53 to 19-23/34-38), but now they won't move further RIGHT. Maybe they've reached their limit?
The gap between eyes was:
- After LEFT: 9-13 and 49-53, gap = 36 cols (13 to 49)
- After RIGHT: 19-23 and 34-38, gap = 11 cols (23 to 34)
So the gap decreased from 36 to 11 with 6 RIGHT presses. But now the gap won't decrease further. Maybe the minimum gap is 11 cols?
Or maybe the eyes can't overlap or touch, and 11 cols is the minimum separation?
Let me think about what the goal could be:
1. Minimize the gap between eyes (get them as close as possible)?
2. Maximize the gap (get them to the edges)?
3. Get the eyes to a specific gap?
4. Something else entirely?
Actually, I just realized something. The eyes are at DIFFERENT row positions:
- Left eye: rows 39-43
- Right eye: rows 44-48
They're not at the same height! Maybe the goal is to get them to the SAME height AND at a specific horizontal position?
Let me try moving the eyes UP or DOWN to align them vertically, then try SPACE.
Actually wait, I already tried this earlier and the eyes ended up at different heights. Let me think about why.
Earlier observation:
- Both eyes at rows 49-53 initially
- After 6 UP: left at 39-43, right at 44-48
- After 5 DOWN: left at 44-48, right at 49-53
- After 5 more DOWN: both at 54-58
So the eyes CAN be at the same height (54-58), but they can also be at different heights. The movement seems to depend on obstacles or constraints.
Let me try getting the eyes to the same height (like 54-58) and then try SPACE.
Actually, I already tried that and it didn't work. Let me try a completely different approach.
What if the goal is related to the bars? The bars are at 59 pixels each (both top and bottom). What if the goal is to get both bars to 64 pixels (full)?
Currently both bars are at 59 pixels. I need to increase them by 5 each. Let me try clicking on the left side (yellow) to increase the bars.
Actually, I've been clicking on various things and the bars have been changing. Let me try a systematic approach:
1. Get the bars to a specific value (like 64)
2. Get the eyes to a specific position
3. Press SPACE
Let me try getting both bars to 64 by clicking on the left side.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# RIGHT doesn't move eyes anymore - they're stuck at current position
# Eyes: left at cols 19-23, right at cols 34-38, different rows (39-43 vs 44-48)

# Let me try a different hypothesis: maybe the goal is about the bars
# Current bars: both at 59 pixels
# Let me try to get both bars to 64 (full) by clicking left side

seg = current_frame.segmentation
w_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] < 100]
print(f"Current W bars: {[n['pixels'] for n in w_nodes]}")

# Click left side to increase bars
for i in range(10):
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 30, 'col': 10}])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    w_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] < 100]
    w_vals = [n['pixels'] for n in w_nodes]
    print(f"Click {i+1}: W={w_vals}, done={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
    if last_action_result.get('level_completed'):
        break
    if w_vals == [64, 64]:
        action(['SPACE'])
        print(f"SPACE at W=64: done={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Current W bars: [64, 64]
Click 1: W=[64, 64], done=False
SPACE at W=64: done=False
